impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers agreeing to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycomb products had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent-law defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of patent holders who engage in tying arrangements can successfully bring antitrust counterclaims to challenge exclusionary licensing practices that extend patent power into unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when conditioning patent licenses on exclusive purchases of unpatented products, particularly where those products have substantial uses beyond the scope of the patent.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 and Alice step one, and whether damages based on foreign sales were properly awarded.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patent claims were directed to an abstract idea at Alice step one and remanding for step-two analysis. The court also ruled that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's sales to customers outside the United States. The decision significantly impacts software and cybersecurity patent enforcement.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing cybersecurity patent infringement claims gain a stronger basis to challenge patent eligibility, and the foreign-sales damages ruling limits exposure for companies with global customer bases.
impacts.negativeImplications: Universities and patent holders asserting software-based cybersecurity patents face heightened § 101 eligibility hurdles, potentially undermining the enforceability of broad classes of computer security patents.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims where the patent applicant possessed prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) describing overlapping compositions and processes but did not disclose them to the PTO.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact. Tenaris acquired prior art documents describing a predecessor product with overlapping characteristics to its patented coiled tubing, raising questions about whether Tenaris deliberately withheld material prior art from the PTO. The case was remanded for trial on both issues.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of infringement gain stronger tools to challenge patents where applicants may have withheld known prior art, particularly in cases involving acquired predecessor technology.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire prior art through corporate asset acquisitions face significant litigation risk if they fail to disclose those materials during prosecution, including potential antitrust liability under Walker Process.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server tailoring content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size or capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's judgment for GoTV and held that the asserted streaming patent claims were ineligible under § 101, entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to a device's capabilities. This ruling required reversal of a $2.5 million jury verdict. The court also vacated the district court's rulings on inducement and damages evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain protection from patent assertions covering broadly claimed content-delivery optimization methods that lack technical specificity beyond the abstract concept.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital content delivery and streaming space face increased difficulty enforcing claims directed to server-side content optimization or device-tailored rendering.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the 30-day deadline to seek a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) may obtain a stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling its district court declaratory judgment action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory deadline for a mandatory stay of parallel district court litigation by dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action after the deadline had passed. The ruling prevents gamesmanship with the § 1659(a) stay provision and preserves the declaratory judgment plaintiff's right to proceed in district court alongside the ITC investigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing ITC investigations retain their ability to pursue parallel district court declaratory judgment actions when ITC respondents miss the statutory stay deadline, preserving access to judicial forums for patent disputes.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day stay deadline under § 1659(a) cannot reset the clock through procedural maneuvering, requiring strict compliance with statutory timing requirements.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement functionality violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches with blood oxygen features that infringed Masimo's patents, upholding the limited exclusion order. The court found no error in the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This is a landmark decision affecting one of Apple's flagship products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the wearable health technology space are validated in their ability to enforce patents through ITC proceedings against major technology companies, including obtaining exclusion orders on high-profile consumer products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating health monitoring features into consumer devices face significant supply chain disruption risk from ITC exclusion orders when those features are covered by third-party patents.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed 'encryption information' in claims covering streaming partly encrypted media content, where the Board's construction led to rejection of Netflix's obviousness challenge.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's patent covering encrypted media streaming, vacated the Board's decision upholding patentability, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision affects patent validity in the adaptive bitrate streaming and digital rights management technology space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies and technology firms challenging patents in IPR proceedings benefit from the Federal Circuit's correction of overly narrow claim constructions that shield patents from prior art challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the encrypted media streaming space face renewed invalidity challenges following claim construction reversals by the Federal Circuit.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on the exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent case against Microsoft involving Azure SmartNIC and VFP technology, vacated summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy, and vacated the denial of Exafer's motion to reopen discovery. The decision reinforces that district courts must carefully evaluate expert methodology rather than reflexively excluding damages opinions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more protective standard for damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that Daubert exclusions will effectively terminate meritorious infringement cases before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face greater difficulty in obtaining summary judgment based solely on the exclusion of a plaintiff's damages expert, as courts are expected to allow more latitude in expert methodology.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced infringement and granting JMOL to the accused infringer of surgical tool patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and its grant of JMOL to DePuy, remanding for a new trial on induced infringement of spinal surgery device patents. The court found the district court improperly excluded expert testimony on how surgeons used the accused tools, which was critical to proving induced infringement of the en bloc derotation patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting method claims against medical device manufacturers benefit from broader admissibility of expert testimony on how end-users employ accused products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed trial exposure when appellate courts reverse expert exclusions, increasing litigation costs and uncertainty in induced infringement cases.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's rejection of Apple's challenge to PTO institution-denial instructions (e.g., the Fintiv framework), holding they are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that IPR institution is discretionary, the Director retains ultimate authority, and the instructions do not bind the Director. This preserves the PTO's broad discretion over IPR institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued discretion to deny IPR petitions under existing policy frameworks without being subject to rulemaking challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and frequent IPR petitioners lose a significant procedural avenue to challenge PTO policies that limit access to inter partes review, particularly in cases involving parallel district court litigation.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether restaurant and business applicants denied grants from the Restaurant Revitalization Fund have Tucker Act jurisdiction to sue the United States for damages when the SBA allegedly failed to award grants in the statutory order of receipt.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the government's motion to dismiss, holding that over 300 restaurants and businesses stated a viable damages claim under the Tucker Act. The statute mandated that the SBA 'shall award grants' in the order applications were received, and the SBA allegedly processed and paid later applicants first. The decision allows a large-scale claim against the government for misadministration of a $28.6 billion pandemic relief program.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses denied federal grant funding due to alleged agency misadministration have a viable path to damages claims against the government when statutes use mandatory language regarding fund distribution.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potentially significant monetary liability for alleged departures from statutory grant-distribution requirements in large-scale pandemic relief programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees beyond what was statutorily authorized to fund the electronic court records system.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million settlement in a nationwide class action alleging that PACER fees were used to fund expenses beyond the operation of the PACER system itself, in violation of the E-Government Act. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representative nonprofit organizations, rejecting all objections raised by an individual objector.
impacts.positiveImplications: Frequent PACER users, including businesses and law firms, will receive reimbursement for excessive fees paid over an eight-year class period, and the settlement establishes boundaries on how the judiciary may use PACER revenue.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities enabling asynchronous viewing of multiple data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google, holding that the asserted web conferencing patent claims failed to state a claim for relief. The decision impacts enforcement of patents claiming methods for asynchronous review of multimedia presentation data streams in web conferencing systems.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating video conferencing and streaming platforms gain greater protection from broadly claimed web conferencing patents that may be directed to abstract ideas.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting web conferencing and multimedia time-shifting patents face heightened pleading standards and eligibility challenges when suing major technology platforms.

impacts.caseName: Fortress Iron, LP v. Digger Specialties, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent is invalid for omission of a coinventor when the patent holder acknowledges the omitted coinventor but cannot locate them to obtain consent for correction under 35 U.S.C. § 256.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that patents were invalid for omitting a coinventor where the patent holder acknowledged the omission but could not locate the missing coinventor (a former employee of a foreign partner) to obtain the consent required under § 256(a). The court also affirmed denial of correction under § 256(b), highlighting the limits of judicial inventorship correction when a coinventor cannot be found.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers can challenge patent validity based on omitted inventors even when the omission was not deceptive, creating a powerful defense when collaborative development involves foreign partners.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that develop products collaboratively with foreign partners face invalidation risk if they fail to track and maintain contact information for all contributors who may qualify as coinventors.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the CIT's ruling that the ITC's blanket confidentiality practice for questionnaire responses in trade remedy investigations is incompatible with the governing statute and does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The decision requires the ITC to apply confidentiality designations consistent with statutory standards rather than automatically treating all responses as confidential.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic competitors gain greater transparency into trade remedy proceedings, potentially enabling better-informed litigation strategies and public accountability in antidumping and countervailing duty cases.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in ITC trade remedy investigations face increased risk that their business information may become publicly accessible, requiring more careful confidentiality designation practices.
